articles.title: From "POS" to “GTFO,” Breaking Down the Meaning of Insult Abbreviations
articles.author: Eden Gordon
articles.excerpt: Abbreviations are useful tools for shortening words and communicating quickly and casually. While people have always used abbreviations and shorthand to communicateâone only needs to do some cursory...
articles.content:
Abbreviations are useful tools for shortening words and communicating quickly and casually. While people have always used abbreviations and shorthand to communicateâone only needs to do some cursory research into old-fashioned slang to see thatâsocial media and the rise of texting have generated a whole new array of slang words, abbreviations, and acronyms that communicate everything from basic concepts to extremely nuanced emotions.Â
Some of these terms are benign, like the iconic LOL, but others are a bit sharper and come in handy whenever you need to express something a bit more intense. The following abbreviations are insults that can either be used humorously in the right context, or that can be genuinely sharp and cruel takedowns.Â
A caveat: itâs important to make sure you know your audience and fully understand the meaning of each these words before using them. If youâre tempted to throw out one of these terms in an online argument, it is also worth remembering that the person on the other side of the screen is a real person with real thoughts and feelingsânot an NPC, as tempting as it might be to call them one when incensed.Â
GTFO
GTFO stands for âget the fuck out,â and is generally used to shut someone else down or to tell them to leave the conversation. It is sometimes also used to express shock or surprise, and can also refer to leaving for a trip or exiting a situation. According to slang lexicographer Jonathan Green, this acronym actually dates back to the 1990s.
POS
POS stands for âpiece of shit.â It is, in short, not a very nice thing to call someone.
IDC
While not exactly always an insult, IDC often functions as a highly dismissive way to simply say, âI donât care.â
TL;DR
TL;DR means âtoo long, didnât read.â This is also not always an insult, but it can serve as a belittling takedown when deposited beneath someoneâs long and heartfelt post or comment. In its non-insulting context, it can signify a summary of a longer text or video.
IDGAF
This abbreviation stands for âI donât give a fuck,â and it is a rather savage way to say you donât care about someone or something at all.
STFU
STFU stands for âshut the fuck upâ and is yet another way to forcefully tell someone to be quiet.Â
GALMA
GALMA means âgo away, leave me alone,â and like many other terms on this list, it is a way of telling someone to get out, stop, cease, log off, or otherwise stop interacting.
SMH
SMH means âshaking my head,â and it can be used in many contexts to express disapproval, disappointment, or sometimes contempt.Â
GOMB
GOMB typically stands for âget off my back,â and is a way to command someone to stop bothering or trolling you. It can also mean âget off my brain,â which is typically said when someone posts something you were just about to write in a group chat or on a comment thread and means, âstop reading my mind.â These meanings are not to be confused with the Hungarian word gomb, which means âbutton,â or the Illinois Governorâs Office of Management and Budget (GOMB).
NPC
NPC stands for ânon-playable character,â and originates from video games, where it refers to characters who canât be controlled or manipulated by the player. As an insult, it can refer to someone who seems to have little independence, autonomy, or critical thinking, and can be used to describe someone who is perceived as boring or overly willing to conform to social or political ideologies.Â
SOB
SOB stands for âson of a bitchâ and is generally a way of insulting someone, technically by way of insulting their mother. Hey, even Shakespeare made âyo mamaâ jokes.
Love trivia? OrderÂ Mental FlossâsÂ new game,Â Brain Brawl, to test your knowledge in a head-to-head tournament style.

articles.title: NASA Report Reveals the Failures That Left Two Astronauts 'Stranded' on the International Space Station
articles.author: Redação
articles.excerpt:
NASA Report Reveals the Failures That Left Two Astronauts ‘Stranded’ on the International Space Station
NASA’s administrator blames both the agency and Boeing for Starliner’s infamous problems
In the...
articles.content:
NASA Report Reveals the Failures That Left Two Astronauts ‘Stranded’ on the International Space Station
NASA’s administrator blames both the agency and Boeing for Starliner’s infamous problems
In the summer of 2024, the plight of two NASA astronauts, Suni Williams and Butch Wilmore, captured the world’s attention. On its first crewed test flight to the International Space Station (ISS), their Boeing spacecraft, Starliner, experienced propulsion system anomalies after launching on June 5, 2024, and the agency ultimately deemed it best for Williams and Wilmore to use a different spacecraft for their return.
As such, Starliner’s homecoming that September was in autonomous mode and the astronauts remained on the ISS. It wasn’t until March 2025 that the astronauts came back via SpaceX’s Crew-9 mission. Williams retired at the end of 2025.
Now, NASA has released a redacted investigation report on the mission’s failures that points the finger at both the agency and its contractor. The report identifies issues ranging from technological to cultural, though it specifies that investigations into the technical causes are ongoing.
“The Boeing Starliner spacecraft has faced challenges throughout its uncrewed and most recent crewed missions,” NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman says in a statement. “While Boeing built Starliner, NASA accepted it and launched two astronauts to space.”
The situation has been determined to be a Type A mishap, the “highest-level classification.” The deadly Columbia and Challenger space shuttle accidents were also Type A mishaps, as reported by the New York Times’ Kenneth Chang.
According to the report, the investigating team found “a complex interplay of hardware failures, qualification gaps, leadership missteps and cultural breakdowns that collectively posed unacceptable risks to crew safety.”
Quick facts: Spaceflight contractors
Boeing—like SpaceX—is contracted to shuttle astronauts to and from low-Earth orbit for NASA in its Commercial Crew Program. SpaceX has been flying people to the ISS in this program since 2020.“Starliner has design and engineering deficiencies that must be corrected,” Isaacman said at a press conference yesterday. “But the most troubling failure revealed by this investigation is not hardware. It’s decision making and leadership that, if left unchecked, could create a culture incompatible with human spaceflight.”
NASA is implementing changes in response to the report, according to the statement.
“These are very complex programs, and complex programs like this fail in complex ways,” Don Platt, director of the Florida Institute of Technology Spaceport Education Center and a former ISS engineer, tells NPR’s Brendan Byrne. “Those organizational issues are, oftentimes, maybe even more important than the technical problems that they’re facing.”

articles.author: Redação
articles.excerpt:
Investigators Unravel $12 Million Ticket Fraud Scheme at the Louvre
Police have arrested nine individuals in connection with the crime, though they have not revealed their identities
It’s been a...
articles.content:
Investigators Unravel $12 Million Ticket Fraud Scheme at the Louvre
Police have arrested nine individuals in connection with the crime, though they have not revealed their identities
It’s been a rough few months for the Louvre: Recurring staff strikes, a high-profile jewel heist, building maintenance issues and, now, the revelation of a massive, long-running scam that officials say may have cost the Paris institution $12 million.
Last week, authorities arrested nine individuals in connection with a Louvre ticketing scheme that may also have affected the Palace of Versailles, reports Reuters.
The investigation began more than a year ago, when museum officials started to suspect tour guides were reusing tickets multiple times. That initial inkling, reported to authorities in December 2024, led to the discovery of a much broader scamming network, per the New York Times’ Ségolène Le Stradic.
In addition to reusing tickets, investigators allege the perpetrators also bribed employees and split up tour groups to avoid paying extra fees. The suspected fraudsters are accused of bringing in up to 20 groups of visitors per day for a decade.
Authorities say they have seized more than $1 million in cash, more than $500,000 from bank accounts, three vehicles and several bank safe deposit boxes. The suspects are also believed to have invested some of the money into real estate in France and Dubai.
Did you know? A crown damaged in the October 2025 Louvre heist will be restored
- The Louvre has assembled a team to oversee the restoration of Empress Eugénie's crown, which the thieves dropped on the sidewalk as they fled the scene of the crime. Officials estimate that the work will cost nearly $50,000.
Officials have not shared the names of the individuals who have been arrested so far. But the group includes two Louvre employees, two tour guides and one individual who is believed to be the mastermind of the scheme, according to Le Parisien’s Flavien Gagnepain, Florian Loisy and Candice Doussot.
Prosecutors have charged the suspects with several crimes, including fraud committed by an organized gang, use of forged documents, corruption and aggravated money laundering.
As prosecutors have been investigating the fraud ring, officials at the Louvre have been making changes intended to prevent similar situations from arising in the future, reports Thomas Adamson of the Associated Press (AP). The museum has restricted the number of times a ticket can be validated at various checkpoints around the building, for instance.
After news of the scheme came to light, Kim Pham, the Louvre’s general administrator and second-in-command behind director Laurence des Cars, told the AP that fraud was “statistically inevitable” for the institution. The Louvre is the most-visited museum in the world and welcomes nearly 9 million visitors per year. Its footprint is also massive, spanning 925,000 square feet.
“Which museum in the world, with this level of attendance, would not at certain moments have some issues of fraud?” Pham said.
He added: “I won’t tell you that we do everything very well and that we did everything well. What I’m telling you is that the fight against fraud is an action of every moment.”
Pham also pointed out that 90 percent of the Louvre’s tickets are sold online, so “that is where major fraud takes place.” The Louvre has also had issues with fraudulent purchases made by stolen cards, the use of fake tickets and free tickets being resold.
The administrator also argued that the daily visitor cap, implemented in June 2022, likely contributed to the rise of scams.
“When you limit the number of people who can enter a museum each day, you increase the scarcity of the ticket and that brings fraudsters,” he told the AP. “It was like for a concert with a star—it’s when places are limited that it creates even more fraud.”

articles.title: Scientists Still Have So Much to Learn About Archaeopteryx, the Dinosaur That May Have Flown Like a Bird
articles.author: Redação
articles.excerpt:
Scientists Still Have So Much to Learn About Archaeopteryx, the Dinosaur That May Have Flown Like a Bird

articles.content:
Scientists Still Have So Much to Learn About Archaeopteryx, the Dinosaur That May Have Flown Like a Bird
A new study suggests features in the prehistoric creature’s mouth helped it eat more efficiently, giving the species the energy needed to go airborne
Around 150 million years ago in the late Jurassic period lived Archaeopteryx, a dinosaur considered an early ancestor of birds that has fascinated paleontologists since it was first discovered in Germany in the 19th century. Ever since a nearly complete Archaeopteryx fossil was acquired by Chicago's Field Museum in 2022, and researchers are now beginning to better understand this remarkable creature.
According to a recent study published in the journal The Innovation earlier this month, the dinosaur may have shared features with today’s birds—extra tongue bones, a sensitive beak, and fleshy bumps on the roof of its mouth. These features, which aren’t found in non-flying dinosaurs, could have helped the birds catch and manipulate food more easily. In turn, this would have allowed allowed them to gather the energy needed to develop flight.
“Archaeopteryx is the oldest dinosaur that we know of to fly using feathered airfoils,” says Jingmai O’Connor, a paleontologist at the museum, to Jay Bennett at Science News. “Because flying takes more energy than walking or running or swimming, then it should have features associated with more efficient feeding.”
O’Connor and her team discovered the features when preparing the fossil, which was fully described in a 2025 paper. They spent over a year chipping away at a layer of limestone to uncover the fossil beneath it. “The only reason these structures in Archaeopteryx’s mouth were found at all is because our preparators were doing such a meticulous job working on this fossil,” says O’Connor in a statement.
During the prep, the team used ultraviolet light to make sure they weren’t damaging any soft tissues, which glow under the light. That’s when two of the preparators spotted a series of glowing bumps on the roof of the fossil’s mouth.
“I remember them calling me over and saying, ‘Jingmai, we found something strange, come look at it,” O’Connor recalls in a statement. “They showed me these tiny, glowing dots, and I had no idea what we were looking at.” After comparing the specimen with bird anatomy, the researchers determined that the dots are similar to oral papillae in modern birds, which are fleshy cones that help grip and guide food down the animal’s throat.
Did you know? What is Archaeopteryx?
- Discovered near Munich, Germany, in 1861, the species is named after the Greek words for "ancient" and "wing"
- While other dinosaurs had feathers to keep them arm, paleontologists believe Archaeopteryx had feathers for flight
- Even though this fossil has been widelly known for more than 150 years, the idea that birds are descended from dinosaurs took more than a century to take hold among scientists.
The team also found other features that had never been seen in Archaeopteryx before. One of them, a tiny tongue bone, would have made the dinosaur’s tongue more flexible. “In the evolution of birds, in response to their increased caloric demands, they evolve a mobile tongue … and they evolve these oral papillae,” O’Connor says to Science News. “Like in living birds, these are structures that work together.”
CT scanning also revealed that the tip of the fossil’s beak had traces of nerves, also a feature seen in modern birds. Bill-tip organs give birds "added sensory information" that help them with different tasks, like looking for food, O’Connor explains to Aristos Georgiou at Live Science.
Overall, the researchers think these three features worked together to help the early birds to feed more efficiently. This, they argue, allowed them to meet the energetic demands needed for flight.
Researchers not involved in the work say that while the new study provides interesting new insight on the anatomy of early bird specimens, more research is needed to confirm the evolutionary link between the discovered features and flight. Christian Foth, a paleontologist at the Museum für Naturkunde in Berlin, tells Live Science that he is not yet convinced by the hypothesis. Michael Pittman, a paleontologist at the Chinese University of Hong Kong, echoes that sentiment to Science News.
“Whether it’s a relationship with flight I would say is very much a working hypothesis. I think we need to do more sampling to be able to support that,” he says to the outlet. “But as a hypothesis, it’s definitely very exciting.”

articles.title: This Massive, Meat-Eating Dinosaur Was a 'Hell Heron' That Waded Into Shallow Waters to Nab Slippery Fish
articles.author: Redação
articles.excerpt:
This Massive, Meat-Eating Dinosaur Was a ‘Hell Heron’ That Waded Into Shallow Waters to Nab Slippery Fish
Paleontologists unearthed a new species of Spinosaurus in the Sahara Desert in Niger, a...
articles.content:
This Massive, Meat-Eating Dinosaur Was a ‘Hell Heron’ That Waded Into Shallow Waters to Nab Slippery Fish
Paleontologists unearthed a new species of Spinosaurus in the Sahara Desert in Niger, a discovery that adds to the debate over whether the prehistoric creatures were fully or semi-aquatic
Roughly 95 million years ago, a large, meat-eating dinosaur with a curved, sword-like crest atop its head prowled around what is now Africa. The fearsome creature had a long snout, interlocking teeth and a sail-like protuberance on its back.
Researchers digging at a remote site in the Sahara Desert in Niger recently unearthed fossils that belonged to the massive animal, which they say is a previously unknown species. They describe the new creature, which they’ve named Spinosaurus mirabilis, in a new paper published February 19 in the journal Science.
S. mirabilis lived during the Cretaceous Period, a time when the now-parched Sahara was covered in trees and rivers. Scientists think the creatures measured roughly 40 feet long, weighed between 10,000 to 14,000 pounds and stood 32 to 46 feet tall.
Did you know? Venturing deep into the Sahara Desert
For the study, paleontologists explored a remote site in the Sahara Desert, where a French geologist had reported discovering a single fossilized tooth in the 1950s. During their first expedition in 2019, the team unearthed a mysterious crest bone, which clued them in to the fact that they might have stumbled upon a new species. When they returned in 2022, they found even more fossils, including two more crests, as well as teeth and jaw bones, and confirmed they'd identified a previously undiscovered dinosaur.
The dinosaur had large, conical-shaped teeth that fit together perfectly, an ideal adaptation for piercing and trapping slippery fish. Its nostrils were also set back on its snout, which likely helped it breathe normally when its mouth was underwater to nab prey.
Though the 20-inch crest atop S. mirabilis’ head is made of solid bone, researchers believe it was probably just for show, because it seems too delicate to have been used for defense. While the dinosaur was alive, the crest was probably covered in keratin—the protein that makes up human hair and nails—and may have been brightly colored. The creatures might have used the structure to identify one another, demonstrate their sexual prowess or establish dominance for territories.
“It’s about love and life—attracting a mate, defending your hot feeding shallows,” says lead author Paul Sereno, a paleontologist at the University of Chicago, to Reuters’ Will Dunham. “What else could be more important?”
The newly discovered species belongs to the genus Spinosaurus, a controversial group of dinosaur predators with just one other known species, Spinosaurus aegyptiacus. Because most S. aegyptiacus fossils come from sites in Egypt and Morocco that once bordered the prehistoric Tethys Sea, some scientists have speculated that Spinosaurus dinosaurs were fully aquatic creatures that spent their lives diving and swimming in open water.
But the new S. mirabilis fossils were discovered much farther inland, between 300 and 600 miles from what would have been the nearest ocean during the Cretaceous Period. The location of the new discovery, coupled with some of S. mirabilis' unique physical characteristics, suggests Spinosaurus dinosaurs may have instead been semi-aquatic hunters that waded into shallow waters to nab large fish—similar to how modern-day herons hunt for food.
“I envision this dinosaur as a kind of ‘hell heron,’” Sereno says in a statement.
However, not everyone is convinced by this semi-aquatic argument. Some scientists note that, because of their large size and relatively short legs, Spinosaurus would have had a difficult time sneaking up on prey as they waded into the water. They also believe the creatures’ bones and tails were optimized for life at sea.
The debate likely won’t be settled for good until researchers discover more Spinosaurus fossils. In the meantime, Matt Lamanna, a paleontologist at the Carnegie Museum of Natural History who was not involved with the research, wonders if the truth lies somewhere in the middle.
“What if it’s doing both?” he tells National Geographic’s Helen Thompson. “What if it’s wading sometimes? What if it’s getting into the water and swimming around some? The common denominator is ambush, whether that was from shore or from the water.”
